rulings.Calendar Matter: ACCOUNTING PETITION RE: Accounting #9, Report, Fees
rulings.Rulings: Continued to 08/28/2024, as the following issues (see tentative rulings for 04/24/2024 & 06/05/2024 hearings) remain unaddressed: 1) No proposed order has been received. 2) No financial account statements verifying cash balances as of 09/20/2023 are on file. 3) The petitioner appears to have paid herself $1,440 as fees on account prior to the conservatee's death, in addition to $10,800 paid during the supplemental account period. The total unpaid balance must be further reduced by $1,440. 4) The ending balance of the supplemental account period stated on the summary of account does not match the ending balance stated on Schedules E & F. 5) A specific amount of net cash distributable to the personal representative of the conservatee's estate, after payment of fees and costs sought to be approved, must be stated in the petition and proposed order. 6) The court-appointed attorney for the conservatee has not petitioned for fees and costs, although her creditor claim was filed in the conservatee's probate estate case more than 2 years ago. Note: An appearance may be required on 08/28/2024 for the Court to consider whether to take this petition off calendar for lack of diligence, unless papers showing satisfactory progress to address the issues are filed.

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE JOHNNY J. AND MARGIE CHERRY TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion By Respondents Jimmy Cherry, Jr And Delphine Cherry Motion For Respondents To Amend, Withdraw And Or Relief From Waiver Of Deemed Admission Responses And Objections
rulings.Rulings: [Part 1 of 5] This tentative ruling is issued by Judge Stephen M. Murphy. Before the court is Respondents Jimmy Cherry, Jr And Delphine Cherry's Motion For Respondents To Amend, Withdraw And Or Relief From Waiver Of Deemed Admission Responses And Objections. Petitioner's Request for Judicial Notice of certain court records is GRANTED, under Evid. C. section 452(d). Judicial notice is limited to the relevant facts properly subject to notice given the materials presented and does not, for example, extend to the truth of assertions in those materials. (See Richtek USA, Inc. v. UPI Semiconductor Corp. (2015) 242 Cal.App.4th 651, 659-660.) Respondents' Motion is DENIED, for the reasons discussed below. Per the Notice of Motion, Respondents ask the Court pursuant to Code of Civil procedure section 2033.280(a) to "grant a motion for respondents to amend withdraw and or relief from waiver of deemed admissions on the grounds that respondents have substantially complied with responses to admissions, there was no bad faith and delay in no prejudice to petitioner so that matter may be Judy [sic] adjudicated and tried on its merits." Code of Civil procedure section 2033.280(a) allows the court, on motion and with sufficient showing, to relieve a party from having waived their right to serve objections to requests for admission. As Petitioner correctly argues in the Opposition, CCP section 2033.280 does not address Respondents' additional requests to withdraw or amend their responses to Requests for Admission, Set 1 and Set 2 ("RFAs") which were deemed admitted in this Court's January 24, 2024 Order Granting Motion for an Order Deeming Matters Specified in Requests for Admission Set 1 and Set 2 Admitted. Code of Civil Procedure section 2033.300 addresses that type of relief. CCP section 2033.300 is not referenced at all in the Notice of Motion, and thus is not properly before the Court for consideration as a grounds for the Motion (see, CCP section 1010, "Notices must be in writing, and the notice of a motion, other than for a new trial, must state when, and the grounds upon which it will be made, and the papers, if any, upon which it is to be based."). However, despite the Motion's procedurally defective notice, the Motion's Memorandum cites Section 2033.300 and a related case, and the Opposition presents substantive argument under Section 2033.300. In the interests of judicial economy in resolving all issues presented in the Motion, and finding no prejudice to the opposing party in doing so, the Court will address the Motion under both CCP section 2033.280 and CCP section 2033.300. Respondents have filed a late Reply to the Opposition, two days before the regularly scheduled hearing on the Motion. This is not the first time Respondents have filed late papers. They have offered no explanation why the Reply was filed late. Accordingly, the Court GRANTS Petitioner's request to strike the Reply and will not consider it. Petitioner's other objections to evidence submitted with the Declaration of Clinton Killian and Memorandum of Points and Authorities are OVERRULED.

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE JOHNNY J. AND MARGIE CHERRY TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Notice Of Motion And Motion By Respondents Jimmy Cherry, Jr And Delphine Cherry Motion For Respondents To Amend, Withdraw And Or Relief From Waiver Of Deemed Admission Responses And Objections (Added For The Purpose Of Posting The Tentative Ruling).
rulings.Rulings: [Part 2 of 5] Under CCP 2033.280, If a party to whom requests for admission are directed fails to serve a timely response, the following rules apply: (a) The party to whom the requests for admission are directed waives any objection to the requests, including one based on privilege or on the protection for work product under Chapter 4 (commencing with Section 2018.010). The court, on motion, may relieve that party from this waiver on its determination that both of the following conditions are satisfied: (1) The party has subsequently served a response that is in substantial compliance with Sections 2033.210, 2033.220, and 2033.230. (2) The party's failure to serve a timely response was the result of mistake, inadvertence, or excusable neglect. To the extent Respondents' Motion relies on section 2033.280, it is DENIED. This statute applies only to relief from waiver of objections and, as Petitioner asserts, is inapplicable in this situation. Under CCP section 2033.300, (a) A party may withdraw or amend an admission made in response to a request for admission only on leave of court granted after notice to all parties. (b) The court may permit withdrawal or amendment of an admission only if it determines that the admission was the result of mistake, inadvertence, or excusable neglect, and that the party who obtained the admission will not be substantially prejudiced in maintaining that party's action or defense on the merits. (c) The court may impose conditions on the granting of the motion that are just, including, but not limited to, the following: (1) An order that the party who obtained the admission be permitted to pursue additional discovery related to the matter involved in the withdrawn or amended admission. (2) An order that the costs of any additional discovery be borne in whole or in part by the party withdrawing or amending the admission. 1. Under CCP section 2033.300, Respondents' admissions to RFAs Set 1 and Set 2 were not the result of mistake, inadvertence, or excusable neglect. On January 24, 2024, this Court entered its Order Granting Motion for an Order Deeming Matters Specified in Requests for Admissions Set 1 and Set 2 Admitted and Imposing Monetary Sanctions. Respondents filed a late opposition to the underlying motion, and attorney Dorianne Romero Plihon appeared to contest the tentative ruling on behalf of Respondents. This Court found Petitioner properly served Requests for Admission Set 1 on Respondents by mail on May 17, 2023. Petitioner timely served Notice of Waiver of Objections on June 24, 2023. Respondents served only late, invalid, non-Code compliant written objections to all of Requests for Admission Set 1 on June 28, 2023, after the statutory time to respond had expired. After meet and confer efforts, Petitioner revised Set 1 and personally served Request for Admissions Set 2 on Respondents on July 6, 2023, though their attorney. There was no response to Set 2, or a Code-compliant response to Set 1. This Court held in the Order Granting Motion, etc., "Petitioner has carried the statutory burden in this Motion. Requests for Admission Set 1 was properly served on the opposing party; the time to respond has expired; and no valid response of any kind has been served. There is no showing Respondents served any valid responses to either Requests for Admission Set 1 of Set 2 prior to the [January 17, 2024] hearing." (Order at 5:5-9.) The Court sanctioned Respondents, finding no substantive justification for their opposition to the motion. The explanations offered in the Motion and declarations of counsel, sworn and unsworn, as to why Respondents failed to validly respond to the RFAs do not support a finding that the failure to serve valid Code-compliant responses to RFAs Set 1 and Set 2 was due to mistake, inadvertence, or excusable neglect.

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE JOHNNY J. AND MARGIE CHERRY TRUST

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE JOHNNY J. AND MARGIE CHERRY TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Notice Of Motion And Motion By Respondents Jimmy Cherry, Jr And Delphine Cherry Motion For Respondents To Amend, Withdraw And Or Relief From Waiver Of Deemed Admission Responses And Objections (Added For The Purpose Of Posting The Tentative Ruling).
rulings.Rulings: [Part 4 of 5] Rather than supporting a finding that Respondents' failure to serve valid Code-compliant responses to RFAs Set 1 and Set 2 was due to mistake, inadvertence, or excusable neglect, counsels' declarations, and the rest of the record before the Court, demonstrates Respondents' counsels' professional incompetence regarding the statutory requirements of the Discovery Act, affirmative and intentional frustration of valid requests for discovery; failure to properly oversee a contract attorney; and actions verging on client abandonment. Finally, Petitioner has argued setting aside the January 24, 2024 Order and allowing Respondents to withdraw and amend the admissions, would substantially prejudice her pending petition and motions. As discussed below, the Court agrees with Petitioner. 2. Under CCP section 2033.300, Petitioner will be substantially prejudiced in maintaining that party's action on the merits if the court allows amendment or withdrawal of the admissions to RFAs Set 1 and Set 2. The First Amended Petition By Windy R. Cherry Concerning the Internal Affairs of a Trust and for Declaratory Relief, etc. was filed and noticed on April 28, 2023. Petition Concerning Internal Affairs of a Trust and for Orders Suspending Trustees And Appointing Windy R Cherry As Interim Trustee and Allocating Costs, was filed April 4, 2024. Both petitions are currently set for hearing on August 7, 2024. Petitioner's motion for summary judgment and motion for summary adjudication, both filed April 18, 2024, are set for hearing July 24, 2024. Per the Opposition, the Petition filed April 4, 2024 and the motions are based on the deemed admissions, and if this Motion is granted, those motions and petition will fail (see, Opposition to Motion, beginning at page 11). Respondents agree, arguing the pending motions will be nullified if the Court allows amendment or withdrawal of the default responses to RFAs Set 1 and Set 2. "Without the procedural deemed admissions [Petitioner's motion for summary judgment] submits absolutely nothing to support its allegations." (Decl. of Killian at 3:2-3.) Under CCP sec. 2033.300(b), "The court may permit withdrawal or amendment of an admission only if it determines that the admission was the result of mistake, inadvertence, or excusable neglect, and that the party who obtained the admission will not be substantially prejudiced in maintaining that party's action or defense on the merits." (emphasis added.) The Court does not find that the default admissions were the result of mistake, inadvertence, or excusable neglect, and finds that Petitioner will be "substantially prejudiced in maintaining" her action on the merits if the Court allows Respondents to amend or withdraw the default admissions to RFAs Set 1 and Set 2, ordered by the Court on January 24, 2024.

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE JOHNNY J. AND MARGIE CHERRY TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Notice Of Motion And Motion By Respondents Jimmy Cherry, Jr And Delphine Cherry Motion For Respondents To Amend, Withdraw And Or Relief From Waiver Of Deemed Admission Responses And Objections (Added For The Purpose Of Posting The Tentative Ruling).
rulings.Rulings: [Part 3 of 5] The sworn declaration of Fannyan states: Romero Plihon was hired by Killian and Fannyan "on a contracted, as needed assignment basis," that this was the pair's first time hiring a contract attorney, "and it appears we did not do it properly;" and that while Killian was ill and Fannyan was transitioning to his separate practice and dealing with emergency personal issues in January 2024, "we prepared the response to the motion to compel and Ms. Romero Plihon made the appearance." (Decl. at par. 2 and 3). Per Fannyan's declaration, the attorneys of record affirmatively decided to oppose the motion to deem RFAs admitted, drafted that opposition, and lost, resulting in the Order deeming the unanswered RFAs Set 1 and Set 2 admitted. This outcome was not the result of "mistake, inadvertence, or excusable neglect." The unsworn declaration of Killian filed May 7, 2024, and his sworn late-filed June 17 Supplemental Declaration, both state: his office associated in Romero Plihon in September 2023, and she alone communicated with Petitioner's attorney beginning October 2023; from January 2024 to March 15, 2024, Killian was ill and unable to perform legal services; Fannyan was unavailable from January 2024 through "most of the month of February" 2024 while the firm began dissolution and Fannyan experienced a personal emergency; his office "went from 4 workers to 0" in the January to February 2024 period; he was under the impression that his office had complied with the January 24, 2024 Order, until he returned to part-time work on March 15, 2024, whereupon he allegedly served Code-compliant responses by mail March 18, 2024. (Killian Decl. at 1:27-28, 2:2-8; 2:14-24.) The attorney of record and an "associated" attorney share the duty of representation. (See, e.g., Streit v. Covington & Crowe (2000) 82 Cal. App. 4th 441, 445-47; Wells Fargo & Co. v. City and Cnty of San Francisco (1944) 25 Cal. 2d 37, 43, "If the attorney of record [ ] associates another attorney with him, it rests with them to divide the duties concerning the conduct of the cause.") Clinton O'Keefe Killian, of Kilian & Fannyan, 1300 Clay Street Suite 600, Oakland, California 94612 is the only attorney of record for Respondents Jimmy Cherry and Delphine Cherry. However, neither Mr. Killian nor an attorney from his law firm appeared at any hearing in this matter until May 15, 2024. Instead, another attorney, from a different law firm, Dorianne Romero Plihon, of Romero Plihon Law Offices, PO Box 2122, Danville, California 94526, appeared at every calling of this matter, purportedly on behalf of Respondents, from July 13, 2023 until June 12, 2024. An unfiled "Notice of Association of Counsel" listing Ms. Romero Plihon and signed by Clinton Killian on behalf of Respondents was attached as Exhibit A to Declaration of Dorianne Romero-Philon in Response to Order to Show Cause, filed January 22, 2024. Per Killian's declarations, he provided no oversight of the contract attorney during the time she was handling the case, was aware that his law partner was leaving the practice and unable to attend to office matters, was aware that he had no support staff, and despite being medically unable to devote the necessary time and attention to providing quality of service for his law practice to protect the interests of Respondents, remained the attorney of record. (see, Bus. & Prof. Code sec. 6190.)

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF MARLENE ANN SHERMAN
rulings.Calendar Matter: ACCOUNTING PETITION RE: Report, Fees, Final Distribution
rulings.Rulings: Grant without hearing. A petition for final distribution must set forth the specific amount of cash on hand and the specific amount to be distributed (CRC 7.651 and LRSF 14.35 I.6.a.), but, because the administrator is the sole heir, this was not required in this case.

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE SUZANNE CASTLE TRUST DATED APRIL 22, 2009
rulings.Calendar Matter: Verified Petition For Order To Set Aside Amendment Of Trust, And Invalidate Will Due To (1) Undue Influence, (2) Lack Of Testamentary Capacity, And (3
rulings.Rulings: Continued to 8/14/24. (No earlier availability.) Issue(s) necessitating continuance: (1) No proposed order has been lodged or received by the assigned examiner. [LRSF 14.7 A. and 14.59 E.] (2) Trusts require 30 days' notice. (3) Notice must have attached to it the procedures for how to make a remote appearance by Zoom and CourtCall, as well as remote public access, as set out at https://sf.courts.ca.gov/divisions/probate-court. [LRSF 14.1 C.6.] (4) Notice must be sent directly to a party entitled to notice unless the party is an adult and has directed in writing that notice be mailed in care of another. [CRC 7.51(a)(2).] (5) This Court requires an ex parte petition to be filed to dispense with notice to a person whose whereabouts are unknown. Please provide a proposed order and courtesy copies to the assigned examiner by 8/2/24. Examiner continuances are final and not subject to contest under LRSF 14.10 B. (As courtesy copies were not received by the examiner, as required by LRSF 2.7 B. and 14.7 B., the petition was reviewed online; there may be additional issues.) NB: Except for exhibits, each paper filed with the court must bear a footer in the bottom margin of each page, placed below the page number and divided from the rest of the document page by a printed line. [CRC 2.110(a).]

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF JONATHAN GEORGE STEVENS
rulings.Calendar Matter: PETITION FOR LETTERS OF ADMINISTRATION
rulings.Rulings: Hearing. Appearance required. The petitioner is a nominee of a non-US resident beneficiary ineligible for her own appointment under Probate Code section 8402(a)(4). The Court is to consider each factor set forth in section 8465(d). The Court is then to determine whether good cause is supported by specific findings of fact under section 8465(e) to waive the petitioner's bond, as the beneficiary's bond waiver does not constitute good cause under that subdivision. PARTIES APPEARING SHOULD CHECK IN FOR THE HEARING WITH THEIR FULL NAME. Counsel and parties may appear personally or remotely through Zoom or CourtCall. For a video appearance, go to zoom.us, click "join a meeting" and input meeting ID 160 225 4765 and password 514879. For an audio-only appearance through Zoom, call 1-669-254-5252 and key in meeting ID "160 225 4765#", then participant ID "0#", then password "514879#". (Toll rates may apply).) The Court prefers that counsel and parties appearing by video input their first and last name into the "Your Name" dialogue box. Counsel and parties may appear by audio-only telephone through CourtCall by calling 1-888-882-6878. Please review LRSF 14.1(C) for more information.
